perturbation.original_text: MKOS agrees that it will put in place, if it has not already done so, policies and procedures relating to and addressing, with the commercially reasonable intent to ensure compliance with, applicable securities laws, rules and regulations, including, but not limited to: A. Disclosure requirements regarding the required disclosure of the nature and terms of UTEK's relationship with, including, but not limited to press releases, publications on its web site, letters to investors and telephone or other personal communication with potential or current investors.
perturbation.changed_text: MKOS agrees that it will endeavor to put in place policies and procedures that generally relate to and address applicable securities laws, rules, and regulations. These policies and procedures may include: A. Considerations for disclosure requirements regarding the nature and terms of UTEK's relationship with MKOS, potentially encompassing press releases, website publications, investor letters, and telephone communications.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity by replacing the commitment to 'ensure compliance' with 'endeavor to put in place' and 'generally relate to,' weakening the obligation. The phrase 'may include' makes the listed actions discretionary rather than mandatory, creating further vagueness regarding MKOS's responsibility to comply with securities laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text requires MKOS to ensure compliance with disclosure requirements under securities laws. By changing the language to 'endeavor to put in place policies' and making specific disclosures optional, the modified text contradicts the mandatory disclosure requirements under Rule 10b-5, which prohibits material misrepresentations and omissions in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original clause is strong because it mandates action to ensure compliance. The altered text is now vague because it weakens the commitment to comply with securities laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'ensure compliance' with 'endeavor to put in place,' weakening the commitment to comply with securities laws. The original text mandated actions to ensure compliance, while the altered text makes specific disclosures optional, creating vagueness and contradicting the intent of Rule 10b-5. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, prohibits omissions of material facts necessary to prevent statements from being misleading in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The modification introduces ambiguity and reduces the obligation to disclose, potentially leading to such omissions, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: MKOS agrees that for a twenty four months (24) following the execution of this Agreement, MKOS shall not, without UTEK's prior written consent, directly or indirectly solicit for employment any present employee of UTEK, or request, induce or advise any employee of UTEK to leave the employ of UTEK. In turn, UTEK agrees that it will not directly or indirectly solicit any present employee of MKOS.
perturbation.changed_text: MKOS should generally avoid soliciting UTEK's employees for a period of two years after this agreement. UTEK, in turn, will also generally avoid soliciting MKOS employees.
perturbation.explanation: The original non-solicitation clause creates a definite obligation. This change makes the obligation ambiguous by replacing 'shall not' with 'should generally avoid,' weakening the prohibition. The addition of 'generally' introduces uncertainty and reduces the enforceability of the clause, as it's unclear what circumstances might justify soliciting employees. This may cause conflicts in states where non-solicitation agreements need to have clear and limited scope.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington State law on non-compete agreements, RCW 49.62.020
perturbation.law_explanation: The original agreement establishes a clear restriction on solicitation. Washington State law regarding non-compete agreements and related restrictions emphasizes the need for clarity and reasonableness. By making the non-solicitation clause ambiguous with 'should generally avoid,' the revised text could be challenged as overly broad or unenforceable under RCW 49.62.020, which requires such agreements to be definite in scope and duration, especially considering the phrase 'generally avoid' is open to interpretation and does not define clear solicitation conduct.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 49.62.020: When void and unenforceable. Print RCWs > Title 49 > Chapter 49.62 > Section 49.62.020 49.62.010 << 49.62.020 >> 49.62.030 PDF RCW 49.62.020 When void and unenforceable. (1) A noncompetition covenant is void and unenforceable: (a)(i) Unless the employer discloses the terms of the covenant in writing to the prospective employee no later than the time of the initial oral or written acceptance of the offer of employment and, if the agreement becomes enforceable only at a later date due to changes in the employee's compensation, the employer specifically discloses that the agreement may be enforceable against the employee in the future; or (ii) If the covenant is entered into after the commencement of employment, unless the employer provides independent consideration for the covenant; (b) Unless the employee's earnings from the party seeking enforcement, when annualized, exceed one hundred thousand dollars per year. This dollar amount must be adjusted annually in accordance with RCW 49.62.040 ; (c) If the employee is terminated as the result of a layoff, unless enforcement of the noncompetition covenant includes compensation equivalent to the employee's base salary at the time of termination for the period of enforcement minus compensation earned through subsequent employment during the period of enforcement. (2) A court or arbitrator must presume that any noncompetition covenant with a duration exceeding eighteen months after termination of employment is unreasonable and unenforceable. A party seeking enforcement may rebut the presumption by proving by clear and convincing evidence that a duration longer than eighteen months is necessary to protect the party's business or goodwill. [ 2024 c 36 s 3 ; 2019 c 299 s 3 .]
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'should generally avoid' weakens the non-solicitation clause, creating an ambiguous legal obligation. This ambiguity directly contradicts the requirement for clarity and definiteness in agreements, as highlighted by the Law Explanation referencing Washington State law (RCW 49.62.020). While the provided snippet of RCW 49.62.020 focuses on conditions under which a noncompetition covenant is void, the underlying principle of the law emphasizes the need for clear terms, which the changed text undermines by introducing ambiguity. The use of 'generally avoid' leaves the scope of the restriction open to interpretation, and would make it difficult to enforce, thus a contradiction exists.
